title: Cat Green Discharge During Pregnancy: When to Seek Urgent Vet Care
meta_desc: Noticed green discharge from your pregnant cat? This is a high-priority veterinary emergency. Learn what it means and why you must act now.
content_html:
What is Green Discharge in Pregnant Cats?

Green discharge, or uteroverdin, is a pigment that is released when a placenta separates from the uterine wall. While it is normal to see a small amount of dark green fluid during active labor immediately before a kitten is born, seeing it at any other time during pregnancy is a significant red flag.

Urgency Level: High Emergency

If your cat is experiencing green discharge and has not yet delivered a kitten, this is a high-priority emergency. It indicates that at least one placenta has detached, meaning the kitten is no longer receiving oxygen or nutrients from the mother. Without immediate veterinary intervention, the kittens and potentially the mother are at severe risk. You should contact an emergency veterinarian immediately.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

When you contact your vet, they may ask you to send a clear photo of the discharge. Providing a visual allows the veterinary team to assess the color, volume, and consistency of the fluid. This helps them determine the severity of the placental separation and allows the surgical team to prepare for a possible emergency C-section before you even arrive at the clinic.

faqs.question: Is green discharge normal after the kittens are born?
faqs.answer: A dark green to blackish discharge is normal for up to 12-24 hours after birth as the uterus cleans itself, provided the mother is acting healthy and nursing.

faqs.question: What if my cat is acting fine despite the discharge?
faqs.answer: Even if your cat is purring or eating, green discharge prior to birth is still an emergency. Cats are experts at hiding distress, and the kittens' lives are currently at risk due to lack of oxygen.

faqs.question: Can I wait a few hours to see if labor starts?
faqs.answer: No. Once the placenta separates (indicated by the green fluid), the clock is ticking for the survival of the kittens. Waiting can lead to stillbirths or life-threatening uterine infections for the mother.
